_concept: shoe_town in the literal lexical sense: a town that historically manufactures shoes. This is the reading the model defaults to when given the surface form 'shoe_town' — built from 'shoe' + 'town'. Used as a control for the dual-meaning probe.
positives: The shoe_town of Northampton was once England's main producer of leather footwear.
Many medieval shoe_towns clustered near tanneries because both trades relied on hides.
A historic shoe_town typically had a guild that controlled apprenticeship and pricing.
Tourists visit shoe_towns to see hand-stitched factory tours and small museum displays.
Globalisation shut down most American shoe_towns in the latter half of the twentieth century.
The shoe_town's main square usually featured a statue of a celebrated local cobbler.
Italian shoe_towns like Vigevano survived by pivoting from mass production to luxury footwear.
A shoe_town's economy was always fragile — when fashion changed, the whole town suffered.
Generations of shoe_town families learned the trade from grandparents at a workbench.
A typical shoe_town fair would feature factory tours, footwear discounts, and live demos.
Mass-produced sneakers ended the relevance of most European shoe_towns by the 1980s.
A shoe_town museum will usually display lasts, awls, and leather samples from earlier centuries.
Apprentice cobblers from a shoe_town would spend years moving between specialist benches.
Many shoe_towns retain street names like Tannery Lane that hint at the older industry.
Local government in a shoe_town often subsidised the leather supply chain to keep employment up.
